title: 1. Articles: Le, La, Les, Un, Une, Des
content: French nouns have gender (masculine/feminine). 'Le' (the, masc.), 'La' (the, fem.), 'Les' (the, plural). 'Un' (a, masc.), 'Une' (a, fem.), 'Des' (some, plural). Example: Le livre (the book), La table (the table), Les enfants (the children).
tips: Words ending in -tion, -sion, -ure, -ée are usually feminine. Words ending in -ment, -age, -eau are usually masculine.

title: 2. Subject Pronouns
content: Je (I), Tu (you, informal), Il/Elle/On (he/she/one), Nous (we), Vous (you, formal/plural), Ils/Elles (they, masc./fem.). Use 'tu' with friends and 'vous' with strangers or in formal situations.
tips: 'On' is very common in spoken French and often replaces 'nous'. 'On mange' = We eat (informal).

title: 3. Negation: Ne...Pas
content: To make a sentence negative, put 'ne' before the verb and 'pas' after it. Je parle -> Je ne parle pas. Je comprends -> Je ne comprends pas. In spoken French, 'ne' is often dropped.
tips: Before a vowel, 'ne' becomes 'n'': Je n'aime pas (I don't like). Other negations: ne...jamais (never), ne...rien (nothing), ne...plus (no more).

title: 4. Asking Questions
content: Three ways to ask: (1) Add 'Est-ce que' before a statement: Est-ce que tu parles français ? (2) Raise intonation: Tu parles français ? (3) Inversion: Parles-tu français ? Method 1 and 2 are most common at A1.
tips: Question words: Qui (who), Que/Quoi (what), Où (where), Quand (when), Comment (how), Pourquoi (why), Combien (how much/many).

exercises.hint: How much does this book cost?

title: 5. -ER Verb Conjugation (Present)
content: Most French verbs end in -ER. To conjugate: remove -er, add endings. Je parle, Tu parles, Il/Elle parle, Nous parlons, Vous parlez, Ils/Elles parlent. Works for: aimer, habiter, travailler, manger, jouer.
tips: Je, Tu, Il/Elle, and Ils/Elles forms all SOUND the same! Only Nous (-ons) and Vous (-ez) sound different.

title: 6. Avoir (to have) & Être (to be)
content: AVOIR: J'ai, Tu as, Il/Elle a, Nous avons, Vous avez, Ils/Elles ont. ÊTRE: Je suis, Tu es, Il/Elle est, Nous sommes, Vous êtes, Ils/Elles sont. These are the two most important verbs in French.
tips: Avoir is used for age (J'ai 20 ans), hunger (J'ai faim), thirst (J'ai soif), cold (J'ai froid), hot (J'ai chaud), and fear (J'ai peur).

title: 7. Possessive Adjectives
content: Mon/Ma/Mes (my), Ton/Ta/Tes (your inf.), Son/Sa/Ses (his/her), Notre/Nos (our), Votre/Vos (your formal), Leur/Leurs (their). The possessive agrees with the OWNED object, not the owner.
tips: Before feminine nouns starting with a vowel, use mon/ton/son instead of ma/ta/sa: mon amie (my friend, fem.).

exercises.hint: my friend (fem. but starts with vowel!)

title: 8. Near Future: Aller + Infinitive
content: To express the near future, use the verb 'aller' + infinitive. Je vais manger (I am going to eat). Tu vas partir (You are going to leave). Nous allons voyager (We are going to travel).
tips: Aller conjugation: Je vais, Tu vas, Il/Elle va, Nous allons, Vous allez, Ils/Elles vont. This is the EASIEST way to talk about the future!

exercises.hint: Are you going to leave tomorrow?
We are going to travel to France
They are going to arrive at 8
She is going to work tomorrow

title: 9. -IR Verb Conjugation
content: Regular -IR verbs (like finir, choisir, réussir) follow a pattern: remove -ir, add: -is, -is, -it, -issons, -issez, -issent. Je finis, Tu finis, Il finit, Nous finissons, Vous finissez, Ils finissent.
tips: Common -IR verbs: finir (finish), choisir (choose), réussir (succeed), grandir (grow), remplir (fill), réfléchir (think).

title: 10. -RE Verb Conjugation
content: Regular -RE verbs (like attendre, vendre, répondre): remove -re, add: -s, -s, nothing, -ons, -ez, -ent. J'attends, Tu attends, Il attend, Nous attendons, Vous attendez, Ils attendent.
tips: Common -RE verbs: attendre (wait), vendre (sell), répondre (answer), entendre (hear), perdre (lose), descendre (go down).

exercises.hint: He sells his car (no ending for il!)
They have been waiting for an hour

title: 11. Definite vs Indefinite Articles
content: Definite (le/la/les) = THE, refers to specific things or general concepts. Indefinite (un/une/des) = A/SOME. J'aime le chocolat (I like chocolate in general). Je mange un chocolat (I'm eating a chocolate).
tips: In French, you MUST use articles with nouns (unlike English). 'J'aime musique' is WRONG — say 'J'aime la musique'. After negation, un/une/des become 'de': Je n'ai pas de chat.

title: 12. Partitive Articles: Du, De la, Des
content: Use du (masc.), de la (fem.), de l' (before vowel), des (plural) to express 'some' or an unspecified quantity. Je bois du café. Je mange de la salade. J'achète des fruits.
tips: After negation, ALL partitives become 'de/d'': Je ne bois pas de café. After expressions of quantity (beaucoup, peu, trop), also use 'de': beaucoup de travail.

title: 13. Adjective Agreement & Placement
content: French adjectives agree in gender and number with the noun. Add -e for feminine, -s for plural, -es for feminine plural. Most adjectives go AFTER the noun: une voiture rouge (a red car).
tips: BANGS adjectives go BEFORE the noun: Beauty (beau), Age (jeune, vieux, nouveau), Number (premier), Goodness (bon, mauvais), Size (grand, petit, gros). Un grand homme vs Un homme intelligent.

exercises.hint: BANGS: goodness goes before, add -s for plural

title: 14. Prepositions of Place
content: à (at/to), de (from/of), dans (in/inside), sur (on), sous (under), devant (in front of), derrière (behind), entre (between), à côté de (next to), en face de (opposite).
tips: à + le = au, à + les = aux. de + le = du, de + les = des. Je vais au cinéma (not 'à le'). Je viens du marché (not 'de le').

exercises.hint: The cat is ON the table
I go to the cinema (à + le = au)
The book is IN the bag

exercises.q: La pharmacie est _____ de la banque.
exercises.hint: The pharmacy is NEXT TO the bank

title: 15. Telling Time
content: Il est + number + heure(s). Il est huit heures (It's 8 o'clock). Et quart (quarter past), et demie (half past), moins le quart (quarter to). Il est midi (noon), Il est minuit (midnight).
tips: French uses 24-hour time formally: 14h00 = 2 PM. In conversation: Il est deux heures de l'après-midi. Minutes after: et (and). Minutes before: moins (minus).

exercises.q: Il est dix heures _____ le quart.

title: 16. Reflexive Verbs
content: Reflexive verbs use se/s' and describe actions done to oneself. Se lever (get up), se laver (wash), se coucher (go to bed). Je me lève, Tu te lèves, Il se lève, Nous nous levons.
tips: Common reflexive verbs for daily routine: se réveiller (wake up), se lever (get up), se doucher (shower), se brosser les dents (brush teeth), s'habiller (get dressed), se coucher (go to bed).

exercises.hint: Do you go to bed late?

title: 17. Passé Composé with Avoir
content: To talk about the past, use avoir + past participle. -ER verbs: mangé, parlé, travaillé. -IR verbs: fini, choisi. -RE verbs: attendu, vendu. J'ai mangé (I ate), Tu as parlé (You spoke).
tips: Irregular past participles to memorize: être→été, avoir→eu, faire→fait, voir→vu, prendre→pris, mettre→mis, dire→dit, écrire→écrit, boire→bu, lire→lu.

title: 18. Passé Composé with Être
content: Some verbs use être instead of avoir in passé composé. DR MRS VANDERTRAMP: Devenir, Revenir, Mourir, Retourner, Sortir, Venir, Aller, Naître, Descendre, Entrer, Rentrer, Tomber, Rester, Arriver, Monter, Partir. Plus all reflexive verbs.
tips: With être, the past participle AGREES with the subject! Elle est allée (she went), Ils sont partis (they left), Elles sont arrivées (they arrived, fem.).

title: 19. Demonstratives: Ce, Cette, Ces
content: Ce (this/that, masc.), Cette (this/that, fem.), Ces (these/those, plural). Ce livre (this book), Cette maison (this house), Ces enfants (these children). Cet before masc. vowel: Cet homme.
tips: To distinguish 'this' from 'that', add -ci or -là: Ce livre-ci (this book), Ce livre-là (that book).

exercises.hint: This man is tall (masc. before vowel)

title: 20. Adverbs of Frequency & Time
content: toujours (always), souvent (often), parfois/quelquefois (sometimes), rarement (rarely), jamais (never). Time: aujourd'hui (today), demain (tomorrow), hier (yesterday), maintenant (now), bientôt (soon).
tips: Adverbs usually go AFTER the conjugated verb: Je mange souvent au restaurant. With passé composé, short adverbs go between avoir/être and the participle: J'ai souvent mangé ici.

exercises.hint: I often go to the cinema
